---
title: "What severity asks instead"
---

## From rejection to post-data assessment

Severity analysis changes the focus. Instead of stopping at “significant” or “not significant”, it asks what the observed result tells us about particular hypothetical effect sizes.

A useful way to express the severity question is:

::: {.key-message}
Given what we observed, which hypothetical effect sizes would probably have produced a stronger result if they were true?
:::

## Why this helps

Suppose an estimated overcharge is statistically insignificant. That alone does not prove that the true overcharge is zero. But the estimate may still be informative.

If the estimate is precise, then large hypothetical overcharges may be difficult to reconcile with the observed result. If the estimate is imprecise, then even sizeable overcharges may remain plausible.

Severity analysis therefore links the interpretation of the estimate to the uncertainty around it.

## The ingredients

A severity assessment typically requires:

- the observed estimate;
- the standard error or other measure of uncertainty;
- the relevant test direction;
- the hypothetical effect size or overcharge claim;
- the probability of observing a result more in line with that claim if the claim were true.

## Visual intuition

::: {.placeholder-figure}
Static figure to add here: observed estimate, uncertainty distribution, hypothetical overcharge claim, and severity area.
:::

## Core interpretation

If a hypothetical overcharge would probably have produced a stronger result than the one actually observed, then the failure to observe such a stronger result counts against that hypothetical overcharge.

If, however, the observed estimate would not be unusual even if the hypothetical overcharge were true, then the data do not strongly rule it out.
